questions.question_text: When a driver leaves the cab of a vehicle transporting hazardous materials, where must the shipping papers be placed?

options.label: A
options.text: In the glove compartment

options.label: B
options.text: On the driver's seat or in a holder mounted on the inside of the driver's door

options.label: C
options.text: In the sleeper berth

options.label: D
options.text: In a sealed envelope on the dashboard

questions.explanation: Under 49 CFR 177.817(e), when the driver is not at the vehicle controls, the shipping papers must be placed on the driver's seat or in a holder mounted to the inside of the driver's door, where they are visible and immediately accessible to emergency responders or inspectors.

questions.question_text: What is the correct sequence for the basic description of a hazardous material on a shipping paper?

options.label: A
options.text: Proper shipping name, hazard class, ID number, packing group

options.label: B
options.text: Hazard class, proper shipping name, packing group, ID number

options.label: C
options.text: ID number, proper shipping name, hazard class, packing group

options.label: D
options.text: Packing group, ID number, hazard class, proper shipping name

questions.explanation: Under 49 CFR 172.202(a), the basic description must appear in this sequence: identification number, proper shipping name, hazard class or division, and packing group. Placing these elements out of order is a violation, even if all the required information is present on the document.

questions.question_text: The emergency response telephone number on a hazmat shipping paper can be a company number that goes to voicemail after business hours, as long as the voicemail greeting includes emergency instructions.

options.label: True
options.text: True

options.label: False
options.text: False

questions.explanation: Under 49 CFR 172.604, the emergency response telephone number must be monitored at all times — 24 hours a day, 7 days a week — by a person who is knowledgeable about the hazardous material being shipped or who has immediate access to such a person. A voicemail system does not meet this requirement, regardless of the greeting content.

questions.question_text: How long must a motor carrier retain copies of hazmat shipping papers after accepting a hazardous materials shipment?

options.label: A
options.text: 6 months

options.label: B
options.text: 1 year

options.label: C
options.text: 2 years

options.label: D
options.text: 3 years

questions.explanation: Under 49 CFR 177.817(f), highway carriers must retain a copy of hazmat shipping papers for at least 1 year after the material was accepted for transport. Shippers have a longer retention requirement of 2 years. Hazardous waste manifests require 3-year retention.

questions.question_text: You are reviewing a shipping paper for a load you are about to accept. The paper lists: "Gasoline, UN1203, 3, PG II, 5,000 gallons, 1 cargo tank." The emergency response phone number is listed as CHEMTREC 1-800-424-9300, and the shipper's certification is printed at the bottom but has no signature. Should you accept this shipment?

options.label: A
options.text: Yes — all the required information is present

options.label: B
options.text: No — the basic description is in the wrong sequence

options.label: C
options.text: No — the shipper's certification is unsigned, and the basic description sequence is wrong

options.label: D
options.text: No — CHEMTREC cannot be used as an emergency response number

questions.explanation: There are two problems. First, the basic description is out of sequence — it should read "UN1203, Gasoline, 3, PG II" (ID number first, then proper shipping name), not "Gasoline, UN1203, 3, PG II." Second, the shipper's certification must be signed under 49 CFR 172.204. An unsigned certification is incomplete. The driver should request corrections before accepting the shipment. CHEMTREC is a valid emergency response number, so that is not the issue.

questions.question_text: When a shipping paper lists both hazardous and non-hazardous materials, which of the following is an acceptable method for identifying the hazmat entries?

options.label: A
options.text: Printing the hazmat entries in a smaller font size

options.label: B
options.text: Placing the hazmat entries on a separate page stapled to the back

options.label: C
options.text: Entering an "X" in a column marked "HM" next to each hazmat entry

options.label: D
options.text: Writing "DANGER" in red ink next to each hazmat entry

questions.explanation: Under 49 CFR 172.201(a)(1), hazmat entries must be distinguished from non-hazmat entries by one of three methods: listing them first, highlighting them, or placing an "X" in a column marked "HM." Using smaller font, separate pages, or writing "DANGER" are not recognized methods under the regulation.
